name: Fayte Pixel
classification_notes: Fayte Pixel renders blackletter letterforms using pixel-based, blocky construction, placing it squarely in the blackletter category with a distinctly retro digital subcategory.
summary: A pixel-constructed blackletter in two styles, Hard and Soft, merging medieval letterform conventions with blocky retro digital aesthetics.
description: Two styles define the family: Hard uses sharp, angular pixel strokes; Soft rounds those same pixels for a slightly warmer result. Both maintain the vertical stress and dense texture associated with blackletter, reinterpreted through a grid-bound, retro-digital construction. Suited to game titles, zines and display contexts where blackletter attitude meets lo-fi pixel culture.
foundry_blurb: That That Type describes Fayte Pixel as a blackletter-inspired pixel font in two styles, Hard and Soft, framing it as a bridge between the tradition of monastery scribes and market stall banners and the aesthetic of retro block and square pixel design, suited to proclamations and digital display.

name: Utility Sans Variable Family
classification_notes: The foundry describes it as semi-mono with a tech-oriented aesthetic, placing it in geometric sans territory with monospaced influences and variable font technology.
summary: A semi-mono geometric sans with a tech-leaning aesthetic, available in both static and variable formats across four weights.
description: Built around semi-monospaced proportions, it aims at projects requiring a clean, futuristic register. The family ships in Light, Regular, SemiBold and Bold, each available as both static and variable fonts, with the variable axis allowing continuous interpolation across that range. Glyph coverage extends to Latin Extended with small capitals, alternate single-storey forms, square variants and a full set of numerator, denominator and scientific inferiors.
foundry_blurb: That That Type describe Utility Sans as leaning into tech, positioning it as a semi-mono typeface designed to give a future-oriented look to any project.

name: Slaab Variable Family
classification_notes: The name and page content confirm a slab serif family; the variable width axis spanning standard to DumbWide widths and geometric proportions place it in the geometric slab subcategory.
summary: A variable slab serif spanning nine weights and five widths, from standard to the deliberately over-extended DumbWide.
description: The family runs 45 static styles across a standard width and four progressively wider cuts, topping out at an exaggerated DumbWide that pushes the proportions into display territory. Nine weights from Hair to Black are available at every width, giving considerable range for titling, editorial and branding work. OpenType features include fractions, ordinals, a two-storey 'a' alternate and a wide I, with the full family also available as a single variable font file.
foundry_blurb: That That Type offers Slaab as a variable family collection with weight and width axes, covering Hair through Black across Standard, SemiWide, Wide, ExtraWide and DumbWide cuts, with OpenType features including fractions, ordinals, scientific inferiors, a two-storey 'a', wide I, subscript and superscript.

name: Lastik Variable Family
classification_notes: Described explicitly as a variable serif font family, with weight as the variable axis spanning Regular through Black in both upright and italic styles.
summary: A variable serif family from That That Type offering a weight axis from Regular to Black across both upright and italic cuts, with broad Latin coverage and a full set of OpenType features.
description: The family ships as both static and variable fonts, covering five weights from Regular to Black with matching italics, all accessible through a single variable axis. OpenType support is thorough, including small capitals, oldstyle figures, tabular and proportional figures, fractions, superscript and subscript, and two stylistic sets for numerals. Latin Extended-A and -B coverage accommodates a wide range of European languages.
foundry_blurb: That That Type present Lastik as a variable serif font family, available in five weights from Regular to Black with italic counterparts, offered as both static and variable font files with extensive OpenType feature support.

name: Kurdis Variable Family
classification_notes: The family presents as a clean, upright sans-serif with no visible calligraphic or humanist detail; its systematic width axis spanning ExtraCondensed to ExtraWide with five weight grades per width suggests a geometric or neo-grotesque construction.
summary: A variable sans-serif spanning six widths and five weights, offered as both static and variable-font formats with weight and width axes.
description: Thirty static cuts run from ExtraCondensed to ExtraWide across five weights from Regular to Black, with a corresponding variable font exposing weight and width axes continuously. OpenType features include fractions, superscript, subscript, scientific inferiors and ordinals, plus a stylistic set. The breadth of width variation makes it practical for layouts that need a single coherent typeface across radically different column widths or display sizes.
foundry_blurb: That That Type offer the Kurdis Variable Family as a collection of 30 static styles spanning ExtraCondensed through ExtraWide in weights from Regular to Black, alongside a variable font with weight and width axes, all with OpenType feature support including fractions, ordinals and stylistic alternates.

name: Flint Script
classification_notes: Classified as script due to its flowing, connected letterforms drawn in a contemporary style; the foundry describes a balance between elegant curves and short angles, characteristic of modern calligraphic scripts.
summary: A contemporary script with a single regular weight that plays elegant curves against short angular strokes.
description: Flint Script is a single-weight contemporary script that sits between calligraphic warmth and geometric restraint, with the foundry citing tension between curves and angles as its defining quality. Six stylistic sets offer meaningful alternate forms across a broad range of characters, extending its usefulness across different design contexts. Latin coverage is thorough, reaching into Extended-A and diacritical marks for broad European language support.
foundry_blurb: That That Type describe Flint Script as a contemporary take on a script font, balancing tension between elegant curves and short angles.

name: Thatura Variable Family
classification_notes: Thatura is explicitly described as a geometric sans family, with construction details such as sharp corners, off-axis letterforms and alternative g designs that place it firmly in the geometric tradition with quirky departures.
summary: A geometric sans with variable weight and a suite of OpenType alternates that let you toggle between restrained and playful character sets.
description: Built on geometric foundations, Thatura offers five named weights from Regular to Black plus a variable font, with stylistic alternates covering sharp ascenders, sharp crotches, a single-storey g, an off-axis e and a simplified u. Small capitals, oldstyle figures, ligatures and full superscript and subscript sets round out a feature-rich package. The result sits between a disciplined geometric workhorse and a display face with personality, depending on which alternates you switch on.
foundry_blurb: That That Type describe Thatura as a geometric sans inspired by a half-remembered typeface, built out with more quirk and considerably more customisation. Users can dial in sharp corners and ascenders, off-axis e forms and playful g designs, adjusting the fun factor up or down to suit the job.

name: Marjorie Variable Family
classification_notes: Marjorie is described explicitly as built for long-form text setting, with restrained personality and a quiet, readable character typical of text serifs designed for body copy.
summary: A text serif from That That Type built for sustained reading, with restrained detail across five weights and a variable axis.
description: Marjorie arrives as That That Type's first typeface intended for body copy rather than display use, covering five weights from Regular to Black each with matching italics, plus a variable font. The family includes small capitals, oldstyle figures, tabular figures, superscripts, subscripts and fractions, giving it a solid feature set for long-form editorial or publishing work. Extended Latin coverage handles a wide range of Western European languages.
foundry_blurb: That That Type describe Marjorie as their first typeface made specifically for setting long blocks of text, noting it has just enough personality to make you notice it in rare moments while otherwise staying out of the way and letting the reader read without distraction.

name: Phluff Variable Family
classification_notes: A contemporary rounded sans-serif with soft terminals throughout; positioned as usable across body and display sizes, aligning it with the rounded grotesque subcategory.
summary: A rounded variable sans in six weights, designed to work from body copy through to display without losing its soft character.
description: Six weights from Regular to Black are offered as a variable font, allowing precise weight interpolation between the static instances. The rounded terminals give it a friendly, approachable quality suitable for branding work and packaging, while the broader glyph set covers Latin Extended for wider language support. OpenType features include small capitals, stylistic alternates for the lowercase 'a' and 't', alternate numeral sets and a full suite of figure styles.
foundry_blurb: That That Type describe Phluff as a contemporary round sans-serif variable font family designed to feel at home as body copy in regular and bold weights and as a display font at more extreme weights. They position it as adding softness to a design without sacrificing elevated design sense, making it suitable for high-end branding and more playful applications alike.

name: Fiyona Variable Family
classification_notes: Fiyona sits closest to humanist sans-serif, blending soft organic curves with sharp structural angles in the shoulder stems of a, h, m, n and distinctive terminal details on capitals, giving it a warm but structured character distinct from geometric or grotesque models.
summary: A variable humanist sans with kicked-out shoulder stems, barbed capitals and a reversed descender on the lowercase y, built around the tension between soft curves and sharp acute angles.
description: Inspired loosely by Pantone's colour-of-the-year language around closeness and warmth, the typeface works through specific structural decisions rather than sentiment. The shoulder stem on a, h, m and n kicks outward before meeting a straight vertical, rejecting the continuous curve found in references like Recoleta in favour of a more resolved structure. Five weights from Regular to Black run as a variable axis, supported by small capitals, discretionary ligatures and four stylistic sets covering alternate g, a, j and arch forms.
foundry_blurb: That That Type describe Fiyona as full of personality, with a kicked-out shoulder stem on characters like a, h, m and n, barbs on E, F, L and T, beaks on C, G and S, and a reversed descender on the lowercase y. The design emerged from a desire to take the softness of fonts like Recoleta and add more structure and strength, balancing round outer corners with sharp acute angles and shifting from a more serious feel at lighter weights to something more playful in the heaviest cuts.

name: Franie Variable
classification_notes: The page presents a broad variable sans-serif with no overt humanist or geometric markers evident from the content; the extensive weight and width range is consistent with a grotesque or neo-grotesque classification.
summary: A variable sans spanning four widths from ExtraCondensed to Regular and nine weights from Hair to Black, each with matching italics.
description: Covering 72 static styles plus a variable font, the family moves across ExtraCondensed, Condensed, SemiCondensed and Regular widths with a weight range that begins at Hair and reaches Black. Every combination carries a corresponding italic, giving designers broad latitude across tight-measure poster work and standard text settings. OpenType features include small capitals, tabular and proportional figures, fractions, ordinals and a stylistic set.
foundry_blurb: That That Type offer Franie Variable as a large family collection encompassing a variable font alongside individual static styles, with weight and width axes controllable across the full range from ExtraCondensed Hair to Regular Black, each style available with its italic counterpart.

name: Neuething Variable Family
classification_notes: Described by the foundry as a go-to sans serif; the variable weight and width axes, clean construction and geometric proportions place it firmly in the geometric sans category.
summary: A variable sans covering six weights and five widths from Regular to UltraExpanded, offered as both static and variable font packages.
description: Spanning 30 static styles across six weights and five widths, the family also ships as a variable font with continuous weight and width axes. Width options run from standard to UltraExpanded, making the family particularly suited to display work where horizontal stretch is needed. OpenType features include stylistic sets, fractions, ordinals and superscript.
foundry_blurb: That That Type describe Neuething Sans as the go-to sans serif font family, comprising 30 styles with variable width and weight axes.

name: Honeychrome Variable
classification_notes: Clean, structured letterforms with a tech-inflected character and geometric underpinning, softened by humanist touches particularly in the wider, heavier cuts.
summary: A variable sans spanning four widths and five weights, shifting from workmanlike utility at Regular to expressive personality at the Black and ExtraWide extremes.
description: Honeychrome covers 21 static styles across Regular, SemiWide, Wide and ExtraWide widths, each running from Regular to Black, all unified in a single variable font. The design carries a restrained tech character at its core weights but loosens noticeably in the heavier and wider settings, where letterforms gain more individual presence. Three stylistic sets for the ampersand, plus a full suite of figure styles and standard OpenType features, round out a practical workhorse with display ambitions.
foundry_blurb: That That Type describe Honeychrome as a practical workhorse at regular to bold weights that gets a bit wild at the ends of the spectrum, with more personality in the black weight and wide widths. They characterise it as having an elevated tech bent with a human feel.

name: Aeion Mono Variable
classification_notes: A fixed-width typeface with a technical, geometric character, designed for code, data and screen use across a variable weight axis from Extra Light to Extra Bold.
summary: A variable-weight monospaced family spanning seven weights from Extra Light to Extra Bold, aimed at technical and screen-based contexts.
description: Running from Extra Light to Extra Bold on a continuous variable weight axis, the family covers basic Latin, Latin Extended-A and a broad set of mathematical and currency symbols. OpenType features include fractions, superscripts, subscripts, scientific inferiors and two stylistic sets offering alternate numeral and letter forms. The glyph set suits code editors, data-dense interfaces and tabular display work where consistent spacing is essential.
foundry_blurb: That That Type presents Aeion Mono as a spaced tech font with a variable weight axis, offering seven named weights alongside OpenType features including fractions, ordinals, scientific inferiors, subscript and superscript, and two stylistic sets.

name: Agharti Variable Family
classification_notes: The consistent stroke widths, neutral letterforms and systematic width and weight axes suggest a geometric or neo-grotesque approach; the sheer breadth of width variants points to a display-oriented workhorse sans.
summary: A large variable sans family spanning seven widths and seven weights, built for layouts demanding both compressed headlines and extended settings.
description: With over 49 static styles plus a variable font covering the full weight and width range, Agharti is a systematic sans designed to work across tight and open measures without switching families. Width grades run from Extra Condensed through to Extra Wide, each carrying a consistent weight ladder from Extra Light to Extra Bold or Black. No italic is offered, which positions it as a display and titling tool rather than a text family.
foundry_blurb: That That Type offer Agharti as a variable family collection combining a full static library with a variable font that spans the entire width and weight space, from Extra Condensed Extra Light through to Extra Wide Extra Bold.

name: Brulia Variable
classification_notes: Brulia is a sans-serif with ink trap detailing, a feature associated with grotesque and neo-grotesque typefaces designed for both text and display optical sizes. The ink traps are a functional and aesthetic characteristic rather than a purely decorative one.
summary: A variable sans-serif with ink traps, offered in Text and Display optical sizes for versatility across scales.
description: Brulia ships as a single variable font with two optical masters, Text and Display, letting it adapt from body copy to headline use. Ink traps are built into the design, giving the letterforms a slightly mechanical character that differentiates it from neutral grotesques. OpenType features include discretionary ligatures, ordinals and superscript, with alternate forms for A, O, a, o and several numerals.
foundry_blurb: That That Type describe Brulia as an ink trap sans-serif variable font, available in Text and Display settings with an adjustable ink trap axis and OpenType support for ligatures, ordinals and superscript.

name: That That New Roman
classification_notes: The name and formal characteristics point to a Times New Roman lineage, placing it squarely in the transitional serif tradition with upright and italic companion cuts.
summary: A two-style serif pair in the Times New Roman tradition, offered in Regular and Italic with extended Latin coverage.
description: Issued as a straightforward Regular and Italic pair, the family draws on the transitional serif conventions associated with Times New Roman. Glyph coverage spans Basic Latin, Latin-1 Supplement, Latin Extended-A and -B, with a handful of OpenType alternates for characters including A, J, O and lowercase a, i, j and o. The offering is modest in scope, suited to text-setting work where a familiar serif structure is required.
foundry_blurb: That That Type describe That That New Roman as a serif type pair, available in Regular and Italic, with the specimen presenting both styles at adjustable sizes alongside a full glyph map and a selection of alternate characters.

name: That That New Pixel Variable Family
classification_notes: A pixel-grid display typeface with a variable softness axis that transitions between hard square terminals and soft rounded corners, placing it firmly in the display category as a novelty/screen-aesthetic font.
summary: A pixel typeface derived from That That New Roman, offering a variable softness axis that shifts letterforms between hard square and warm rounded terminals.
description: Four static styles cover Square, Round, Italic Square and Italic Round, while variable font versions let users dial between sharp pixel edges and softened corners along a single axis. The extended Latin character set includes diacritics, currency symbols, fractions and mathematical operators, making it more practical than most novelty pixel fonts. Best suited to screen-forward design work, gaming interfaces and retro-digital branding where some control over warmth is useful.
foundry_blurb: That That Type describe the font as a pixel version of That That New Roman with considerably more flexibility. The variable softness axis lets users add warmth and roundness to what would otherwise be a cold pixel font, and the family ships in four static styles: Roman Hard, Roman Soft, Italic Hard and Italic Soft.

name: Hanno
classification_notes: Described explicitly as a mid-century modern sans serif, placing it in the geometric sans tradition with the even stroke weights and open forms typical of that era.
summary: A single-weight geometric sans drawn from the mid-century modern tradition, covering Latin Extended with a clean, even stroke.
description: Hanno is a geometric sans in the mid-century modern vein, released by That That Type in a single Regular weight. Coverage extends across Basic Latin, Latin-1 Supplement and Latin Extended-A, giving it a reasonable reach across Western and Central European languages. At present there are no additional weights, italics or condensed cuts, making it a focused offering suited to display and branding work where one style is enough.
foundry_blurb: That That Type present Hanno as a mid-century modern sans serif font, available in Regular weight with broad Latin glyph coverage.

name: Comba
classification_notes: Self-described as a quirky display sans serif, Comba is an ultra-wide bold display face with idiosyncratic letterforms suited to headlines and branding rather than text use.
summary: A single-weight ultra-wide display sans with deliberately quirky letterforms, built for headlines and bold graphic applications.
description: Comba comes in one style only: Bold Ultra Wide, with an x-height of 580 and ascenders stretching to 1000, giving it a pronounced vertical presence despite its width. Character-level alternates are available for A, J, O and several others, offering some compositional flexibility. Coverage extends across Latin Extended-A and B with a full set of diacritics, making it usable across a reasonable range of European languages.
foundry_blurb: That That Type describe Comba as a quirky display sans serif font, presented in a Bold Ultra Wide style and intended for expressive, large-scale use.

name: Kish
classification_notes: Kish is explicitly described as a Western reverse contrast font, where horizontal strokes are heavier than verticals, a hallmark of nineteenth-century display wood type traditions. This places it firmly in the display category with a reverse contrast subcategory.
summary: A reverse contrast display face drawing on nineteenth-century Western wood type traditions, released in a single Bold Ultra Wide style.
description: Kish applies the reverse contrast principle characteristic of Victorian-era wood type, where horizontal strokes outweigh vertical ones to create a visually arresting inversion of normal stroke logic. The single Bold Ultra Wide cut suggests use in headline and poster contexts where strong character presence is the primary requirement. Latin Extended character coverage broadens its applicability across Western European languages.
foundry_blurb: That That Type describe Kish as a Western reverse contrast font, offered in a Bold Ultra Wide style and available to buy directly from the foundry.

name: Fuuld
classification_notes: Described explicitly as an ink-conscious sans serif, meaning its design accounts for ink spread at smaller sizes, a trait associated with ink-trap or ink-conscious grotesque construction.
summary: A single-weight ink-conscious sans serif covering an extended Latin character set with OpenType support for fractions, ordinals and superscript.
description: Designed with ink spread in mind, Fuuld draws on the tradition of ink-trap construction to keep letterforms crisp at small sizes and under printing pressure. It ships in one weight with a broad Latin character set that includes Latin Extended-A and -B coverage, making it serviceable across Western and Central European languages. OpenType features include fractions, ordinals, superscript and a small set of alternates for characters such as J, i and j.
foundry_blurb: That That Type describe Fuuld as a sans serif ink-conscious font, with a single Regular style and a specimen showcasing its glyph set and OpenType feature support.

name: World Peace
classification_notes: The typeface is explicitly categorised as Display by the foundry, with a single decorative style suggesting expressive, headline-oriented use rather than text setting.
summary: A single-weight display face offered as a free font, covering basic Latin and built for headline use.
description: World Peace is a display typeface from That That Type, available as a free font. It ships in a single Display style with standard Basic Latin coverage, making it a straightforward choice for expressive headline and poster work. The limited character set and single weight suggest a focused, occasion-specific design rather than a workhorse family.
foundry_blurb: That That Type offer World Peace as a free font, released in a single Display style. The page presents it as a display typeface with Basic Latin glyph coverage.

name: Komique Sans
classification_notes: Described as a hand-written font and offered in a single Display style, placing it firmly in the display category with an informal, handcrafted character.
summary: A handwritten display typeface with a single Display style, covering Latin alongside a set of doubled-letter ligatures.
description: A casual, hand-drawn display face from That That Type offered in one Display weight. Its glyph set covers Basic Latin, selected punctuation and a range of doubled-letter ligatures such as BB, CC and ffi. The informal character suits packaging, editorial headers or branding where a handcrafted quality is wanted without full script complexity.
foundry_blurb: That That Type present Komique Sans as a hand-written font available in a single Display style, with OpenType features and a glyph set that includes standard ligatures and basic Latin coverage.

name: Virgil Mono Variable Collection
classification_notes: Described by the foundry as a variable monospaced typeface with harsh, angular letterforms, placing it squarely in the monospaced category with a geometric, display-adjacent character.
summary: A variable monospaced typeface with deliberately harsh, angular forms intended for contemporary graphic design work.
description: Five weights from Light to Bold are offered as a variable font, giving designers fluid control across the weight axis. The letterforms are sharply geometric with an intentional angularity that distinguishes it from neutral coding or text mono faces. Glyph coverage spans Latin Extended-A and includes standard OpenType features such as ligatures, superscripts and currency symbols.
foundry_blurb: That That Type describe Virgil as a variable monospaced typeface that is harsh, angular, and suited to contemporary design.

name: Mackerol
classification_notes: Mackerol is explicitly described as a reverse contrast sans serif, a display-oriented genre where stroke stress is inverted so horizontal strokes are thicker than verticals, placing it firmly in the display category.
summary: A single-weight reverse contrast sans serif with inverted stroke stress, suited to display and editorial applications.
description: Reverse contrast construction flips conventional stress so horizontal strokes carry more weight than verticals, giving the face an unusual optical tension. The character set covers Latin Extended-A and a range of alternates via stylistic sets, fractions, ordinals and superscripts. At present only one weight is available, limiting its range but keeping the focus on its structural conceit.
foundry_blurb: That That Type present Mackerol as a reverse contrast sans serif, offering a single Regular style with OpenType features including stylistic alternates, fractions, ordinals and superscript figures.

name: Orvil
classification_notes: Orvil is self-described as a bubble font, a display category defined by rounded, inflated letterforms with no calligraphic or serif tradition behind them. It is primarily suited to decorative and headline use rather than text setting.
summary: A single-weight bubble display font with inflated, rounded letterforms and a set of double-letter ligatures for repeated characters.
description: Orvil is a bubble-style display face offered in one weight, covering basic Latin and Latin Extended-A with a handful of OpenType features including ordinals and standard ligatures. Notably, it includes ligatures for doubled letters such as BB, CC, OO and others, which adds visual cohesion when such combinations appear. Best suited to logotypes, packaging and headline work where a soft, rounded personality is the brief.
foundry_blurb: That That Type describe Orvil simply as a bubble font, available in a single Regular style with OpenType feature support including ordinals and access to alternates for selected characters.

name: Yerk
classification_notes: Described explicitly as a Y2K font, placing it in the display category with a retro-futurist aesthetic typical of late-1990s and early-2000s digital type design.
summary: A three-style Y2K display face from That That Type, available in Regular, Italic and Slant cuts.
description: A compact Y2K-styled display typeface offered in three styles: Regular, Italic and a Slant variant. The glyph set covers Basic Latin and Latin-1 Supplement with Western European diacritics, alongside standard OpenType features including ordinals, superscript and standard ligatures. The aesthetic leans into early-2000s digital type trends, suited to branding or editorial work seeking period-specific nostalgia.
foundry_blurb: That That Type describes Yerk as a Y2K font available in three styles: Slant, Regular and Italic.

name: Tumb
classification_notes: Self-described as a quirky display font; the single-weight Latin offering and expressive character forms place it firmly in the display category with no body-text intent.
summary: A single-weight quirky display font covering basic Latin and extended character sets, with no additional weights or styles.
description: Tumb is a display typeface from That That Type, offered in one weight with coverage across Basic Latin, Latin-1 Supplement and a modest selection of Latin Extended glyphs. The character set includes standard ligatures, basic mathematical operators and currency symbols, making it functional for headline and short-form display use. Its single-weight, single-style offering limits its range considerably.
foundry_blurb: That That Type describe Tumb as a quirky display font, positioned for expressive and decorative headline use.

name: Wetris
classification_notes: Wetris is a display typeface described as a liquid font, with eight stylistic sets offering alternate letterforms, placing it firmly in the decorative/experimental display category with no conventional text use intended.
summary: A single-weight display typeface with a liquid aesthetic and eight stylistic sets offering distinct alternate letterform variants.
description: Built around a fluid, liquid visual theme, Wetris ships with eight OpenType stylistic sets that each provide a full alternative uppercase alphabet, giving designers considerable formal variety from a single font file. The character set covers basic Latin with both upper and lowercase. Suited to titles, logotypes and packaging where an expressive, organic quality is wanted.
foundry_blurb: That That Type describe Wetris as a liquid font, with a specimen showcasing eight distinct stylistic sets each offering alternative uppercase letterforms across the basic Latin character set.

name: Baou
classification_notes: Explicitly described as a wide display sans serif; the single Display style and broad proportions place it firmly in the display category with no text weight utility.
summary: A wide, single-style display sans from That That Type, built for large-scale headline and poster use.
description: Baou is a wide display sans serif offered in a single Display style, covering Basic Latin, Latin-1 Supplement and Latin Extended-A character sets. Its broad proportions and high cap height make it suited to headlines and poster work where width and presence matter. The single weight limits its versatility but keeps its character focused.
foundry_blurb: That That Type describe Baou as a sans serif wide display font, positioning it for expressive large-scale typesetting.

name: Gunter
classification_notes: Labelled a display font by the foundry, with a single Regular weight and stylistic alternates suggesting a decorative, headline-focused design rather than a text workhorse.
summary: A single-weight display serif with stylistic alternates and broad Latin Extended coverage, built for headline use.
description: A display serif offered in one Regular weight, with a stylistic set of alternate letterforms for capital letters and a handful of lowercase characters. The character set covers Latin Extended-A and -B, including diacritics for a wide range of European languages. Ordinals and standard ligatures round out the OpenType feature set, keeping it lean but functional for titling work.
foundry_blurb: That That Type presents Gunter as a display font with a single Regular style, offering stylistic alternate glyphs and broad Latin language support.

name: Mostest Mono
classification_notes: Explicitly named and designed as a monospaced font, with all glyphs sharing a fixed width. The numeric weight naming system (550 through 2200) suggests a variable or multi-weight mono family.
summary: A monospaced typeface from That That Type offered across seven numeric weights from 550 to 2200.
description: Mostest Mono is a fixed-width typeface covering Basic Latin, Latin-1 Supplement and general punctuation including currency symbols. Seven weights are available, named numerically from 550 to 2200, giving a wide range from light to heavy. The glyph set is functional and lean, suited to coding interfaces, tabular data or any setting where consistent character width is required.
foundry_blurb: That That Type presents Mostest Mono as a spaced font available in seven weights, ranging from 550 to 2200, with a full set of Basic Latin characters, punctuation and currency symbols.

name: Fayte
classification_notes: Explicitly described as a condensed blackletter font; the glyph set and style description confirm a blackletter category with a condensed cut as the primary offering.
summary: A condensed blackletter display face covering Latin Extended with a single Regular weight.
description: Fayte is a condensed blackletter designed for display use, offered in a single Regular weight. The character set covers Basic Latin, Latin-1 Supplement and portions of Latin Extended-A, giving it reasonable reach across Western European languages. Its condensed proportions make it suited to posters, logotypes and editorial headings where a tight blackletter texture is needed.
foundry_blurb: That That Type presents Fayte as a condensed blackletter font, shown in a single Regular style and available to purchase directly from the foundry.

name: Hideaki
classification_notes: Described explicitly as a semi-condensed serif; the page confirms a single upright serif style with no italic or width variants mentioned, sitting in the condensed serif subcategory.
summary: A single-weight semi-condensed serif covering Latin Extended-A with a modest set of alternates and ligatures.
description: Available in one weight, Hideaki is a semi-condensed serif with a cap height of 750 units and a relatively tall ascender, giving it a vertical, upright texture suited to display or editorial headings. Glyph coverage reaches Latin Extended-A with support for Western European languages, and the font includes a handful of stylistic alternates for A, O, a, o and several numerals alongside standard fi and fl ligatures. With no italic and no additional weights, the family is intentionally minimal.
foundry_blurb: That That Type describes Hideaki as a semi-condensed serif font, available as a single Regular style.

name: Orbit
classification_notes: Self-described as a Y2K display font, Orbit is a geometric display typeface with a wide variant, designed for headline use at large sizes rather than extended text setting.
summary: A Y2K-styled geometric display face with a wide variant, built for large-scale headline use.
description: Orbit leans into the Y2K aesthetic with geometric letterforms suited to posters, logotypes and editorial headlines. The family offers a standard and a Wide variant, with OpenType features including ordinals, superscripts and alternate glyphs for A, O, a, o and numerals. Latin coverage spans Basic Latin through to Latin Extended-A with diacritics.
foundry_blurb: That That Type describe Orbit as a Y2K display font, available in standard and Wide styles.

name: Tickerbit
classification_notes: Tickerbit is a pixel-based typeface with a monospaced variant, placing it squarely in the mono category with a pixel/bitmap subcategory reflecting its grid-constrained construction.
summary: A pixel-based typeface in four styles covering proportional and monospaced cuts, each with a matching italic.
description: Built on a pixel grid, Tickerbit comes in four styles: regular, italic, mono and mono italic. The proportional and monospaced variants share the same pixel aesthetic but allow different typesetting contexts, from running text to code or tabular display. Glyph coverage spans Basic Latin, Latin-1 Supplement and Latin Extended, along with punctuation, currency symbols and mathematical operators.
foundry_blurb: Tickerbit is described by That That Type as a pixel-based typeface available in four styles: regular, italic, mono and mono italic.

name: Vows
classification_notes: The foundry explicitly describes Vows as an elegant condensed display serif, with tall ascenders, tight proportions and a high cap height suited to headline use rather than text setting.
summary: A single-weight condensed display serif with tall proportions and an elegant character suited to headline and editorial use.
description: Vows is a condensed display serif from That That Type, offered in one Regular weight with a tall cap height and generous ascenders that lend it a slender, upright formality. The glyph set covers Basic Latin, Latin-1 Supplement and selected Latin Extended characters, along with standard ligatures for ff, ffi and ffl. Its narrow stance and refined detailing make it well suited to wedding stationery, magazine headlines and branding work that wants a formal, restrained voice.
foundry_blurb: That That Type describe Vows as an elegant condensed display serif, positioned for use cases that call for refinement and verticality in display settings.

name: Cesso
classification_notes: Described by the foundry as a ligature-rich serif display font; the extensive discretionary ligatures and alternate character sets mark it as a decorative display face rather than a text serif.
summary: A single-weight display serif built around an unusually large set of discretionary ligatures and stylistic alternates.
description: Cesso offers one weight but compensates with an extensive OpenType feature set: dozens of uppercase and lowercase discretionary ligatures, two full stylistic sets, and a wide range of alternate letterforms. The ligature pairs range from common combinations like fi and fl through to multi-letter sequences such as THE and RAE, giving designers considerable latitude in headline and logotype work. Coverage extends across Latin Extended-A with diacritics for Western European languages.
foundry_blurb: That That Type describe Cesso as a ligature-rich serif display font, emphasising its broad collection of OpenType features including discretionary ligatures, stylistic sets, and alternate glyphs.

name: OKIE DOKO Variable
classification_notes: Self-described as a Western Style Reverse Contrast Display Font, with strokes thickened horizontally rather than vertically, a hallmark of Clarendon-influenced Western display types.
summary: A variable reverse-contrast display face in the Western tradition, offered in three width variants from Regular to Skinny.
description: Built as a variable font with a width axis running from Regular through Slim to Skinny, Okie Doko draws on the reverse-contrast letterforms associated with nineteenth-century Western wood type. The glyph set covers Latin Extended-A with OpenType features including ordinals, superscripts and access to alternate characters for A, O, a, o and numerals. Suited to headlines, poster work and branding where a period-inflected Western character is called for.
foundry_blurb: That That Type describe Okie Doko as a Western Style Reverse Contrast Display Font, available as a variable font with Regular, Slim and Skinny styles.

name: Fluta
classification_notes: Described explicitly as a Y2K inspired variable display font; the style references late-1990s and early-2000s digital aesthetics rather than any classical typographic tradition.
summary: A variable display typeface drawing on Y2K visual culture, available across five weights from Thin to Black.
description: Built as a variable font spanning five weights, Fluta takes its aesthetic cues from the late-1990s and early-2000s digital era. The glyph set covers Basic Latin and Latin-1 Supplement with a selection of extended accented characters, OpenType features including ordinals, superscripts and standard ligatures, and a handful of alternates for A, O and numerals. Positioned squarely at display use, it has no italic or condensed cuts.
foundry_blurb: That That Type describe Fluta as a Y2K inspired variable display font, available in five weights from Thin to Black.

name: DigiBop
classification_notes: Described explicitly as a Y2K-inspired display font, placing it firmly in the retro display category with digital and late-1990s aesthetic influences.
summary: A Y2K-inspired display face offered in Regular and Italic, drawing on the digital aesthetics of the late 1990s.
description: Built around the visual language of Y2K design, this display font arrives in just two styles: Regular and Italic. Glyph coverage spans Basic Latin and Latin-1 Supplement with a handful of extended Latin characters, plus OpenType features including superscript, subscript, scientific inferiors and ordinals. Best suited to headline and poster use where a late-nineties digital flavour is the point.
foundry_blurb: That That Type describe DigiBop as a Y2K-inspired display font, available in Regular and Italic styles.

name: YE
classification_notes: The foundry classifies it as Display, and the extensive discretionary ligature set, alternate characters and single Display style all point firmly toward a display-oriented typeface rather than a text family.
summary: A single-weight display typeface from That That Type, notable for an unusually extensive set of discretionary ligatures and stylistic alternates.
description: Available in one Display style, YE is built around expressive typographic detail rather than family breadth. Its discretionary ligature set is particularly extensive, covering multi-letter combinations from common pairs like fi and fl through to constructed words such as LOVE, THE and WWW. Stylistic sets and access-all-alternates options for characters including a, e, g and r give designers room to tune the voice of the text.
foundry_blurb: That That Type presents YE as a Display typeface with a broad glyph set covering Basic Latin, Latin-1 Supplement, Latin Extended and standard punctuation, alongside a rich library of discretionary ligatures, standard ligatures, stylistic sets and alternate characters.

name: Lastik Free Font
classification_notes: The page reveals a clean Latin-extended sans with standard grotesque proportions and OpenType features typical of a neutral grotesque; no ornamental or geometric traits are described.
summary: A single free-weight grotesque sans from That That Type, covering Latin Extended with standard OpenType features.
description: A no-cost grotesque sans offering Basic Latin and Latin Extended-A coverage alongside a handful of OpenType features including oldstyle figures, fractions and ordinals. With only one weight available and no italic or condensed cuts, it is best suited to simple display or body-text experiments rather than sustained typographic work. The glyph set is functional and broad enough for Western European languages.
foundry_blurb: That That Type offer Lastik as a free font download, featuring a complete Basic Latin and Latin Extended-A character set with OpenType features including lining and oldstyle figures, fractions, ordinals, superscript and case-sensitive forms.

name: Topoline Variable Font Collection
classification_notes: Topoline is a variable display typeface with named stylistic extremes ranging from Negative (outline/reversed) to Full (filled), suggesting a geometric or constructed letterform approach used primarily for titling and expressive work.
summary: A variable display typeface from That That Type that moves along an axis from hollow outline to solid fill, with five named stops including Negative, Light, Empty, Bold and Full.
description: Rather than varying weight in a conventional sense, Topoline uses its variable axis to shift between different states of fill, from a reversed or negative treatment through to a completely solid letterform. The five named styles give designers distinct visual registers within a single font file. Coverage is limited to basic Latin, numerals and general punctuation, placing it firmly in display and titling territory.
foundry_blurb: That That Type present Topoline as a variable font collection with five distinct styles: Negative, Light, Empty, Bold and Full, each representing a different fill state of the same underlying letterform, available as a single variable font purchase.
